name: The Pigeonhole Principle
whatIsIt: The PIGEONHOLE PRINCIPLE says: if you put more items than boxes, at least one box holds two or more items. Formally, if n items go into k boxes and n > k, some box has ≥ 2. The GENERAL form: with n items in k boxes, some box holds at least ⌈n/k⌉ (round-up) items. Simple to state, but it PROVES surprising 'there must exist' facts.
whyNeeded: Many Olympiad problems ask you to prove something must exist without constructing it. Pigeonhole is the cleanest tool for that: choose the right 'boxes' and counting forces the conclusion.
realLifeUses: Proving two people in a big city have the same number of hairs
Showing in any 13 people two share a birth month
Guaranteeing a repeated remainder in a list of numbers
Proving some pair in a group are friends or strangers

vocabulary.meaning: The things being placed (the larger count).
The categories they fall into (the smaller count).
Round-up of n ÷ k — the guaranteed minimum in the fullest box.

story.title: The Sock Drawer
story.text: Kabir's dark drawer has black and blue socks — just 2 colours. 'How many must I grab to be SURE of a matching pair?' Mummy says, 'Think boxes: 2 colours = 2 boxes. Grab 3 socks — 3 pigeons in 2 holes — so two must share a colour. Three socks guarantee a pair!' Kabir laughs: 'I don't even need to see the colour — counting proves it.'
story.extractedProblem: With socks of only 2 colours, how many must you take to be sure of a matching pair?
story.answerInStory: 3 — 3 socks in 2 colour-boxes force two into the same box (a pair).

grids.label: 3 socks into 2 colour-boxes → one box gets 2 (a pair)

visual.caption: More pigeons than holes means at least one hole is shared. Here 3 socks in 2 colour-boxes guarantee a matching pair.

grids.label: 13 people, 12 months → two share a month
8 people, 7 weekdays → two share a birthday-weekday

standardMethod.summary: Identify the ITEMS (pigeons) and the CATEGORIES (holes). If items > holes, at least two share a hole. For 'at least m in one box', use n items in k boxes forces ⌈n/k⌉ in the fullest.
steps: Decide the boxes (holes) — the categories items fall into.
If items > holes, some box has two or more.
For a stronger count, the fullest box holds at least ⌈items ÷ boxes⌉.

workedExamples.problem: 13 people are in a room. Must two share a birth month?
steps: Yes — at least two share a birth month.

workedExamples.problem: Socks come in 2 colours. Fewest to grab to guarantee a matching pair?
steps: Need one box to hold 2 → grab 2 + 1 = 3.

commonMistakes.mistake: Mixing up which are the items and which are the boxes.
commonMistakes.fix: Items (pigeons) are what you place; boxes (holes) are the categories. You need MORE items than boxes.

commonMistakes.mistake: Grabbing as many as the number of boxes and expecting a repeat.
commonMistakes.fix: With k boxes you need k + 1 items to FORCE a repeat (k could all be different).

easy.q: Socks of 2 colours: fewest to grab for a sure matching pair?
Pigeonhole: more items than boxes means…

medium.q: To guarantee two people share a birth month, fewest people needed?
Colours = 3. Fewest socks to guarantee a matching pair?

challenge.q: 5 items into 2 boxes: the fullest box has at least how many? (⌈5/2⌉)
To guarantee two people share a weekday of birth, fewest people?

questions.q: 2 colours: fewest socks for a sure pair?
With k boxes, to FORCE a repeat you need…
13 people, 12 months: how many at least share a month?
7 items into 3 boxes: fullest box has at least ⌈7/3⌉ = ?

teachBackPrompt: With a grown-up, use socks of 2 colours. Explain why grabbing 3 GUARANTEES a matching pair (3 pigeons, 2 holes). Then explain why 13 people must share a birth month (13 pigeons, 12 holes).

revisionCard.summary: Pigeonhole: n items in k boxes, n > k ⇒ some box has ≥ 2. To force a repeat you need k + 1 items. General form: the fullest box holds at least ⌈n/k⌉. Choose the boxes cleverly to prove 'must exist' facts.

rememberIt.hook: More pigeons than holes ⇒ some hole is shared
rememberIt.unpack: k boxes need k+1 items to force a repeat. Fullest box ≥ ⌈n/k⌉.

studentQuestions.q: How do I choose the boxes?
studentQuestions.a: Pick categories so that a repeat gives what the problem wants — remainders mod m, colours, coordinates, or sums. The right boxes make the answer fall out.
It's n ÷ k rounded up. It tells you the guaranteed minimum load of the fullest box, e.g. 7 items in 3 boxes force a box with 3.

formulas.name: Pigeonhole (basic)
formulas.formula: n items, k boxes, n > k ⇒ some box ≥ 2
formulas.whenToUse: Proving a repeat or a shared category must exist.

formulas.name: Pigeonhole (general)
formulas.whenToUse: Guaranteeing at least m in one box.
